message:
Update E2E action tray tests and beads state

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>

files.patch:
@@ -148,7 +148,7 @@
 {"id":"process_triage-a50","title":"Add 'pt update' command to CLI","description":"## Purpose
Add the update command to pt's CLI interface, integrating all the update infrastructure.

## Parent Epic
Self-Update Mechanism (process_triage-097)

## Depends On
- Implement atomic file replacement (final step in chain)

## CLI Interface

### Commands
```bash
pt update           # Check and install updates
pt update --check   # Check only, don't install
pt update --force   # Update even if on latest version (re-install)
```

### Help Text Addition
```
  update          Check for and install updates
    --check       Check only, don't install
    --force       Force re-installation of current version
```

## Implementation

### Command Handler
```bash
cmd_update() {
    local check_only=false
    local force=false
    
    # Parse arguments
    while [[ $# -gt 0 ]]; do
        case "$1" in
            --check|-c)
                check_only=true
                shift
                ;;
            --force|-f)
                force=true
                shift
                ;;
            *)
                log_error "Unknown option: $1"
                return 1
                ;;
        esac
    done
    
    show_header
    
    log_step "Checking for updates..."
    
    local latest
    if latest=$(check_for_update); then
        log_info "Update available: ${VERSION} → ${latest}"
        
        if [[ "$check_only" == "true" ]]; then
            log_info "Run 'pt update' to install."
            return 0
        fi
        
        # Confirm update
        if [[ "$IS_TTY" == "true" ]]; then
            if \! gum_confirm "Install update?"; then
                log_info "Update cancelled."
                return 0
            fi
        fi
        
        # Do the update
        do_update "$latest"
        
    elif [[ "$force" == "true" ]]; then
        log_info "Forcing re-installation of v${VERSION}"
        do_update "$VERSION"
        
    else
        log_success "Already on latest version ($VERSION)"
    fi
}
```

### Integration in Main
```bash
main() {
    ensure_gum
    ensure_config
    
    case "${1:-}" in
        run|"")      shift 2>/dev/null || true; cmd_run "$@" ;;
        scan)         shift; cmd_scan "$@" ;;
        history)      shift; cmd_history "$@" ;;
        clear)        shift; cmd_clear "$@" ;;
        update)       shift; cmd_update "$@" ;;  # ← Add this
        help|-h|--help)
            cmd_help
            ;;
        version|-v|--version)
            printf 'pt version %s\n' "$VERSION"
            ;;
        *)
            log_error "Unknown command: $1"
            log_info "Run 'pt help' for usage."
            exit 1
            ;;
    esac
}
```

### Update Output Example
```
╭─────────────────────────────────────────────╮
│  Process Triage v1.0.0                      │
╰─────────────────────────────────────────────╯

→ Checking for updates...
ℹ Update available: 1.0.0 → 1.1.0

Install update? [Y/n] y

→ Downloading pt v1.1.0...
✓ Checksum verified: a3f2b8c9d4e5...
✓ Script validation passed
→ Installing update...
✓ Updated to pt v1.1.0

ℹ Run 'pt --version' to verify.
```

## Error Cases
- Network error: "Could not check for updates. Check your connection."
- Checksum mismatch: "Update aborted: checksum verification failed"
- Permission denied: "Cannot write to /path. Try with sudo."
- Syntax invalid: "Downloaded file failed validation, aborting"

## Success Criteria
- [ ] `pt update` checks and installs
- [ ] `pt update --check` only checks
- [ ] `pt update --force` re-installs current version
- [ ] Clear progress messages
- [ ] Graceful error handling
- [ ] Non-interactive mode works (no prompts)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Implements the full scope described above (no silent omissions).
- [ ] Handles edge/error cases described above with deterministic behavior.
- [ ] Includes unit tests for the core logic and the highest-risk edge cases.
- [ ] Emits structured logs/telemetry for key decisions and failures (as applicable).

## Test Plan
- Unit: golden tests for core logic; edge cases and error paths.
- Integration: exercise real tool/system interactions where applicable (or mocked equivalents).
- E2E: cover at least one representative user/agent workflow when applicable.
- Logging: tests validate presence/shape of critical log/telemetry fields.

 {"id":"process_triage-a6q","title":"Code Organization & Standards","description":"## Overview
Code organization and standards for the `pt` bash wrapper and auxiliary scripts (installer, self-update, tool installation).

This epic exists to keep the wrapper:
- readable and maintainable,
- safe (no prompt hangs; clear error remediation),
- automation-friendly (semantic exit codes; stderr vs stdout discipline).

It is intentionally **not** about the premium TUI experience (that lives in `pt-core`).

## Standards
- Clear section markers for auditability.
- Semantic function naming (e.g., `cmd_*`, `ensure_*`, `log_*`).
- Stable exit codes and structured error messages with remediation hints.
- XDG-compliant paths for wrapper state where applicable.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Wrapper scripts follow the standards above and pass ShellCheck.
- [ ] Exit codes and error messages are stable and documented.

## Success Criteria
- [ ] Wrapper changes are safe to iterate on because tests + linting catch regressions.
- [ ] Operational failures (install/update/tooling) are debuggable from logs alone.

 {"id":"process_triage-agz","title":"Define capabilities cache schema","description":"## Task
Specify the schema for caching detected system capabilities and tool availability.

## Background
The wrapper (pt bash) performs capability discovery and passes results to pt-core. This enables:
- Core to make decisions based on available tools
- Graceful degradation when tools are missing
- User awareness of what's available vs missing
- Conditional probe selection

pt agent capabilities returns:
- Platform info (OS, kernel, container status)
- Data sources (procfs, sysfs, perf, eBPF, etc.)
- Supervisors detected (systemd, launchd, pm2, Docker)
- Available actions (kill, pause, renice, cgroup ops)
- Permission level (own user, sudo, root)
- Resource limits (overhead budget, probe limits)
- Feature flags (galaxy-brain, community signatures)

## Deliverables
- JSON schema for capabilities cache
- Detection protocol for each capability
- Version information for detected tools
- Safe fallbacks when tools missing
- Refresh/invalidation strategy
- Per-action capability requirements

## Technical Considerations
- Cache location: ~/.cache/pt/capabilities.json
- Include detection timestamp for staleness
- Some capabilities are runtime-dependent (sudo may work sometimes)
- Container detection affects many capabilities
- macOS SIP affects tracing capabilities

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Implements the full scope described above (no silent omissions).
- [ ] Handles edge/error cases described above with deterministic behavior.
- [ ] Includes unit tests for the core logic and the highest-risk edge cases.
- [ ] Emits structured logs/telemetry for key decisions and failures (as applicable).

## Test Plan
- Unit: golden tests for core logic; edge cases and error paths.
- Integration: exercise real tool/system interactions where applicable (or mocked equivalents).
- E2E: cover at least one representative user/agent workflow when applicable.
- Logging: tests validate presence/shape of critical log/telemetry fields.

Types include OsInfo, ToolInfo, ProcFsInfo, CgroupInfo, SystemdInfo, LaunchdInfo, PsiInfo, ContainerInfo, SudoInfo, UserInfo, PathsInfo, SystemInfo, PrivilegesInfo. Cache load/save to ~/.cache/pt/capabilities.json with staleness checking. All 25 pt-common tests pass.","compaction_level":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"process_triage-agz","depends_on_id":"process_triage-2l3","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-15T09:10:28.086969342Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","metadata":"","thread_id":""}]}
-{"id":"process_triage-aii","title":"EPIC: Testing Infrastructure and Quality","description":"## Overview
EPIC for comprehensive testing infrastructure ensuring correctness and reliability across all components.

## Background
The plan specifies rigorous testing for a system that makes kill decisions. Testing is critical because errors could destroy user work. This EPIC covers unit tests, integration tests, property tests, and benchmarks.

## Why It Matters
Process triage decisions are irreversible. A false positive kills a useful process; user data may be lost. Testing verifies correctness of the math, evidence collection, and decision logic before any process is harmed.

## Scope
1. Unit tests for all pure functions (math, parsing)
2. Integration tests for evidence collection
3. Property-based tests for Bayesian inference
4. End-to-end tests for CLI workflows
5. Performance benchmarks with regression detection
6. Mock infrastructure for isolated testing

## Testing Principles
- Fast: Unit tests run in seconds
- Isolated: No dependency on real processes for unit tests
- Comprehensive: Cover edge cases and error paths
- Reproducible: Deterministic results, seeded randomness
- Documented: Tests serve as executable specification

## Coverage Targets
- Math utilities: 100% line coverage
- Evidence collection: 90% line coverage
- Inference engine: 95% branch coverage
- CLI: All commands and flag combinations

## CI Integration
- All tests run on every PR
- Benchmark comparison against main branch
- Coverage reports with trend tracking
- Mutation testing for test quality

## Success Criteria
- All tests pass reliably
- Coverage targets met
- Benchmarks detect regressions
- Testing enables confident refactoring

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Implements the full scope described above (no silent omissions).
- [ ] Handles edge/error cases described above with deterministic behavior.
- [ ] Includes unit tests for the core logic and the highest-risk edge cases.
- [ ] Emits structured logs/telemetry for key decisions and failures (as applicable).

## Test Plan
- Unit: golden tests for core logic; edge cases and error paths.
- Integration: exercise real tool/system interactions where applicable (or mocked equivalents).
- E2E: cover at least one representative user/agent workflow when applicable.
- Logging: tests validate presence/shape of critical log/telemetry fields.

 {"id":"process_triage-aii.1","title":"Implement E2E action-tray tests (pause/throttle/renice/cgroups) with detailed logging","description":"## Overview
Add end-to-end tests specifically for the **expanded action space** (Plan §6) and the **staged execution protocol** (observe/mitigate before kill), with strong, structured logging to diagnose failures.

These tests complement the general CLI E2E bead by focusing on the highest-risk integration surface: action planning + policy gating + execution + verification.

## Scenarios (must be covered)
### 1) Pause → observe → resume
- Spawn a parent+child workload
- Apply pause to PGID
- Verify CPU drops and process remains present
- Resume and verify recovery

### 2) Throttle (cgroup CPU) → verify relief
- In an isolated test cgroup, throttle CPU
- Verify CPU usage drops (within tolerance)
- Restore previous settings

### 3) Renice → verify scheduling change
- Renice a CPU burner
- Verify nice value changes and is logged

### 4) Kill staged escalation
- SIGTERM with grace period
- If still alive, SIGKILL
- Verify termination and no PID-reuse mixups

### 5) Safety gates (robot mode)
- Ensure robot mode refuses actions when:
  - protected patterns match
  - data-loss gate triggers
  - identity revalidation fails
  - blast-radius caps exceeded

## Logging requirements
- Capture:
  - the generated plan (JSON)
  - every action attempt (structured)
  - verification results
  - failure recovery hints
- On failure, print a minimal replay bundle reference (session artifact dir) to aid debugging.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Tests cover the scenarios above on supported CI environments (or skip with explicit capability checks).
- [ ] Failures produce actionable logs (plan + action outcomes + verification).
- [ ] Tests are deterministic (bounded timeouts, controlled fixtures).

## Test Plan
- E2E: run in CI with capability detection (skip cgroup tests when unsupported).
- Local: provide a script to run these tests with verbose logging.

 {"id":"process_triage-aii.2","title":"E2E: .ptb bundle export/import integrity tests (profiles, checksums, redaction)","description":"## Purpose
Add end-to-end tests that prove `.ptb` bundles are:
- portable
- integrity-checked (manifest + checksums)
- redaction-safe
- reproducible (importing the bundle recreates the same session summary/report surface)

These tests are intentionally **black-box** at the CLI boundary: they validate what users/agents actually rely on.

## Plan requirements covered
From Plan §3.6 + §11:
- `.ptb` is a single-file export container (ZIP default) with:
  - `manifest.json` (schema versions, tool versions/capabilities, host fingerprint, redaction policy version, checksums)
  - `plan.json`
  - telemetry partitions (profile-dependent)
  - optional `raw/` (capped + redacted)
  - optional `report.html` (single file)
- Export profiles:
  - `minimal` (plan + summary only)
  - `safe` (derived features/inference; aggressive redaction)
  - `forensic` (more raw; still policy-redacted; optional encryption handled separately)
- Import must validate checksums and **fail closed** on mismatch.

## Test scope
### 1) Round-trip correctness by profile
For each profile (`minimal`, `safe`, `forensic`):
- Generate a fixture session.
- `pt agent export --profile <profile> --out fixture.ptb`.
- Re-import/read the bundle (via the bundle reader or `pt agent show/report` path).
- Assert:
  - `manifest.json` exists and includes required version fields.
  - `plan.json` exists and matches the original plan’s stable fields (session_id, candidate identities, recommended actions).
  - The report surface renders (either embedded report.html or generated from bundle contents) without missing required fields.

### 2) Integrity failure (tamper tests)
- Flip a byte in the bundle.
- Assert import fails with a deterministic error code and an explicit “checksum mismatch” classification.

### 3) Redaction safety regression tests
Using fixtures containing known sensitive strings:
- Ensure secrets/PII **do not appear** in:
  - `plan.json`
  - `manifest.json`
  - `report.html` (if included)
  - any embedded raw artifacts for `safe`

### 4) Size/overhead sanity
- Large fixture (500–1000 synthetic processes) exports within reasonable size bounds.
- Export time is logged and bounded (regression alert when it grows).

## Fixtures
- A small deterministic fixture with a mix of:
  - orphan-with-supervision
  - zombie remediation case
  - a high-blast-radius server process (to ensure the bundle contains risk metadata)
- A large deterministic fixture for performance.

## Logging requirements (tests)
On failure, tests must log:
- profile
- bundle size
- checksum list and which checksum failed
- redaction scan hits (matched patterns)
- import error classification and stderr

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Round-trip export/import works for `minimal`, `safe`, and `forensic` profiles.
- [ ] Tampered bundles fail closed with clear error classification.
- [ ] Redaction invariants hold across bundle contents.
- [ ] Size/time metrics are recorded for regression tracking.

## Test Plan
- E2E: CLI round-trips and tamper tests.
- Property-ish: generate multiple fixtures with randomized sensitive strings and assert redaction.

 {"id":"process_triage-aii.3","title":"E2E: Dormant daemon (ptd) tests (overhead, triggers, cooldown, inbox)","description":"## Purpose
Add end-to-end tests for **dormant mode** (always-on guardian) so we can trust:
- it stays low-overhead,
- it triggers when it should,
- it does not spam/loop,
- it produces the correct artifacts (session + inbox entry), and
- it never violates safety defaults.

This is explicitly required by Plan §11 (“Dormant daemon tests: low overhead, trigger correctness, cooldown/backoff behavior, escalation produces a session + inbox entry”).

## Background (Plan §3.7 + §10 Phase 8/daemon-related + §11)
Dormant mode is special because it runs continuously; a bug here can degrade the user’s machine or flood them with noise. Tests must therefore validate both correctness **and** resource/operational behavior.

## Scope
### What we are testing
1. **Overhead budget**
   - CPU and memory usage of daemon loop under idle conditions.
   - No unbounded log/event growth.

2. **Trigger correctness**
   - When sustained evidence thresholds are met, daemon escalates to create a triage session.
   - Trigger windows are robust to transient spikes (EWMA + sustained windows).

3. **Cooldown / backoff**
   - After a trigger, cooldown prevents repeated sessions for the same condition.
   - Failure paths use backoff (no tight crash loops).

4. **Artifact + inbox correctness**
   - A trigger produces:
     - durable `session_id`
     - artifact directory (plan + samples + derived + telemetry pointers)
     - inbox item that is actionable by humans/agents

5. **Coordination invariants**
   - Respects per-user `pt lock` (does not race manual runs / agent runs).
   - Does not compete for heavy probes when lock is held (queues inbox instead).

6. **Safety invariants**
   - Dormant mode is advisory-only by default (no auto-kill).
   - Any “suggested” actions are gated by policy and do not execute.

## Test Harness Requirements
- Provide a **test mode** for daemon execution:
  - controllable time source (fake clock) to test cooldown/backoff deterministically
  - deterministic fixture inputs for trigger conditions
  - explicit “single tick” execution for unit-style control

- Provide process fixtures:
  - synthetic process set generator OR controlled lightweight fixture processes
  - ability to simulate signals (CPU deltas, memory growth, orphaning) without requiring privileged ops

## Concrete Test Cases (minimum)
- `test_ptd_idle_low_overhead`
  - Run daemon in idle mode for N ticks; assert CPU/mem within budget; assert no unbounded file growth.

- `test_ptd_trigger_creates_session_and_inbox`
  - Feed sustained trigger evidence; assert session artifacts created; inbox item produced with correct session_id.

- `test_ptd_cooldown_prevents_repeat_sessions`
  - Trigger once; continue feeding evidence; verify no second session until cooldown expires.

- `test_ptd_backoff_on_failure`
  - Force an internal failure (simulated tool timeout / write error); assert backoff increases; no tight loop.

- `test_ptd_respects_pt_lock`
  - Hold the lock (simulate active manual run); feed trigger evidence; daemon must queue inbox item (or defer) without performing heavy work.

- `test_ptd_escalation_ladder_notifications`
  - Validate notification/escalation ladder behavior (quiet → notify → high urgency), including dedupe.

## Logging Requirements (tests)
- Every test prints a structured “why” block on failure:
  - trigger threshold inputs, computed EWMA/window state
  - cooldown/backoff counters
  - session_id + artifact paths
  - inbox payload summary
  - measured overhead metrics (CPU %, RSS, IO bytes)

- Capture daemon logs and assert presence of:
  - `ptd.tick.start/end`
  - `ptd.trigger.evaluated`
  - `ptd.trigger.fired`
  - `ptd.cooldown.active`
  - `ptd.inbox.enqueued`

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All tests above exist and pass reliably.
- [ ] Tests are deterministic (fake clock / fixed fixtures) and do not require root.
- [ ] Logs on failure are sufficient to diagnose “why did it trigger?” or “why didn’t it trigger?”

## Dependencies
- Dormant daemon epic: `process_triage-b4v`
- Daemon core loop: `process_triage-nh7p`
- Inbox command surface: `process_triage-iqe`
- Session model/artifacts: `process_triage-qje`
- Escalation/notifications: `process_triage-a3h0`, `process_triage-1k6`

 {"id":"process_triage-sj6.2","title":"Implement pause/resume actions (SIGSTOP/SIGCONT) with group-aware targeting","description":"## Overview
Implement the **pause** and **resume** actions from Plan §6:
- pause: SIGSTOP (PID or process group)
- resume: SIGCONT

These actions are core to the staged execution protocol: pause/observe is often safer than kill.

## Key Plan Constraints
- Pause is reversible but not free: can stall services, hold locks, and block dependent processes.
- Pause must be gated behind blast-radius checks and session-protection checks.

## Requirements
### 1) Targeting semantics
- Support targeting:
  - single PID
  - process group (PGID) when children exist or when PID is part of a job/workload tree
- Prefer group-aware operations when child processes exist (avoid leaving orphans).

### 2) Safety gates (must be enforced)
- Identity revalidation immediately before signaling: at minimum `(pid, start_id, uid)`.
- Default same-UID restriction.
- Session safety: protect the active user session chain (shell, tmux/screen, ssh) by default.
- Blast radius / dependency gate: do not pause high-impact processes unless explicitly confirmed (and never in robot mode unless policy allows).

### 3) Verification
- After pause: verify state reflects stop (best-effort; OS-specific).
- After resume: verify the process can run again.
- Record outcomes and timing to telemetry.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Pause/resume work for PID targets.
- [ ] Pause/resume work for process-group targets.
- [ ] All safety gates are enforced and logged.
- [ ] Post-action verification and telemetry are recorded.

## Test Plan
- Integration: spawn a controlled CPU burner process + child worker; pause group and verify CPU drops; resume and verify recovery.
- Safety: ensure session-protection blocks pausing the current shell/tmux.
- E2E: staged plan includes pause step and reports after-action diff.
- Logging: verify structured action outcome records include durations and verification results.

 {"id":"process_triage-sj6.3","title":"Implement kill actions (SIGTERM→SIGKILL) with PID+process-group support and TOCTOU safety","description":"## Overview
Implement the **kill** action from Plan §6:
- staged signals: SIGTERM with grace period → SIGKILL as last resort
- target types: PID or process group (PGID)

This is the most destructive action and must be conservative by default.

## Requirements
### 1) Targeting semantics
- If the target has children (or is a known workload tree), prefer group-aware kill (PGID) to avoid leaving orphans.
- Support kill-tree semantics where available (but always derived from verified identity, not stale PID lists).

### 2) Safety gates (non-negotiable)
- Identity revalidation immediately before sending any signal:
  - at minimum `(pid, start_id, uid)`; include `boot_id` where possible.
- Default same-UID enforcement; cross-UID kill requires explicit policy + privileges.
- Protected process denylist must hard-block.
- Data-loss gate integration: open write handles inflate loss and can hard-block robot mode.
- D-state and zombie handling:
  - zombies cannot be killed directly; route to parent remediation.
  - D-state processes may not respond; prefer investigation; avoid repeated SIGKILL loops.

### 3) Verification + outcomes
- Verify termination outcome (process absent OR identity mismatch indicates PID reuse) and record results.
- Detect respawn (supervisor) and emit `supervisor_conflict` failures.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] SIGTERM then SIGKILL escalation works for PID targets.
- [ ] Group-aware kill works and avoids leaving orphans.
- [ ] Identity revalidation blocks PID-reuse races deterministically.
- [ ] Outcomes/respawn detection are recorded in telemetry.

## Test Plan
- Integration: spawn a parent+child tree; kill via PGID and verify all exit.
- Safety: simulate PID reuse via mocked snapshots and ensure kill is blocked.
- Supervisor: mock respawn loop and ensure failure_type is `supervisor_conflict`.
- Logging: verify full audit trail for signals, wait times, and verification results.

Basic data-loss gates implemented; full lock-pattern detection (dvi.1) is separate enhancement.","compaction_level":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"process_triage-sj6.3","depends_on_id":"process_triage-3nz","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-15T10:12:32.882615994Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","metadata":"","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"process_triage-sj6.3","depends_on_id":"process_triage-cfon.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-15T10:12:32.629959936Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","metadata":"","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"process_triage-sj6.3","depends_on_id":"process_triage-dvi.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-15T10:12:32.712529702Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","metadata":"","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"process_triage-sj6.3","depends_on_id":"process_triage-gic","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-15T10:12:32.798789795Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","metadata":"","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"process_triage-sj6.3","depends_on_id":"process_triage-sj6","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-15T10:02:52.548781541Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","metadata":"","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"process_triage-sj6.4","title":"Implement renice action (priority adjustment) with privilege checks and verification","description":"## Overview
Implement the **renice** action from Plan §6: adjust process scheduling priority as a reversible mitigation step when kill is too risky.

## Requirements
### 1) Action semantics
- Support renice for:
  - PID
  - process group (optional; if supported)
- Record the previous nice value and the new nice value (reversibility).

### 2) Safety + privilege
- Enforce same-UID by default.
- Handle permission failures cleanly (`permission_denied`) and attach failure recovery tree.
- Policy must bound how aggressive renice can be (e.g., do not allow negative nice without explicit privilege + allowlist).

### 3) Verification
- Verify the nice value changed (best-effort per platform) and log the outcome.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Renice action can be applied and verified for same-UID processes.
- [ ] Permission denied produces a structured failure type and recovery hint.
- [ ] Prior nice value is captured for reversibility.

## Test Plan
- Integration: spawn a CPU burner, renice it, verify scheduling priority changes.
- Negative tests: attempt to renice without privilege and verify `permission_denied` handling.
- Logging: ensure before/after nice values appear in action outcomes.

-{"id":"process_triage-sj6.5","title":"Implement cgroup freeze/quarantine action (cgroup v2 freezer) with fallbacks","description":"## Overview
Implement the **cgroup freeze/quarantine** action from Plan §6:
- Use cgroup v2 freezer when available (or the closest equivalent).
- Intended as a reversible containment action that is often less disruptive than SIGSTOP for complex workloads.

## Requirements
### 1) Capability detection
- Detect whether cgroup v2 freezer is available and writable.
- If unavailable, downgrade to safer alternatives per policy (e.g., CPU throttle or renice) and record the downgrade.

### 2) Action semantics
- Identify the process’s effective cgroup.
- Apply freeze to the appropriate scope (PID’s cgroup or a quarantine subgroup) without freezing unrelated processes.
- Capture reversibility metadata (unfreeze instructions).

### 3) Safety
- Enforce same-UID by default.
- Gate behind blast-radius checks and protected-process checks.
- Avoid freezing core system services; prefer supervisor-aware stop/restart for supervised services.

### 4) Verification
- Verify frozen state where possible (best-effort; cgroup state indicators).
- Log outcomes and duration.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Freezer action works on cgroup v2 systems where permitted.
- [ ] Fallback path is deterministic when freezer is unavailable.
- [ ] Freezing does not accidentally capture unrelated workloads.

## Test Plan
- Integration: in a cgroup v2-capable test environment, create a test cgroup, place a PID, freeze/unfreeze and verify behavior.
- Negative tests: permission denied and missing freezer controller.
- Logging: ensure downgrade and unfreeze hints are recorded.
","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"Dicklesworthstone","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-15T10:03:16.480157743Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-15T21:20:58.549803547Z","compaction_level":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"process_triage-sj6.5","depends_on_id":"process_triage-3nz","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-15T10:12:42.563640388Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","metadata":"","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"process_triage-sj6.5","depends_on_id":"process_triage-cfon.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-15T10:12:42.483353751Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","metadata":"","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"process_triage-sj6.5","depends_on_id":"process_triage-qa9","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-15T10:12:42.398955733Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","metadata":"","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"process_triage-sj6.5","depends_on_id":"process_triage-sj6","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-15T10:03:16.481934904Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","metadata":"","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"process_triage-sj6.5","depends_on_id":"process_triage-urd","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-15T10:12:42.316426112Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","metadata":"","thread_id":""}]}
-{"id":"process_triage-sj6.6","title":"Implement cgroup CPU throttle action (cgroup v2 cpu.max) with safety gates","description":"## Overview
Implement the **cgroup CPU throttle** action from Plan §6:
- cgroup v2: adjust `cpu.max` (quota/period)
- cgroup v1 (optional): `cpu.cfs_quota_us` / `cpu.cfs_period_us`

This is a reversible mitigation action intended to reduce load while preserving process state.

## Requirements
### 1) Capability detection
- Detect cgroup version and whether CPU controller settings are writable.
- If not supported, downgrade deterministically (renice/pause) per policy.

### 2) Action semantics
- Apply throttling to an appropriate scope so only the target workload is constrained.
- Capture previous settings for reversibility.
- Policy must bound throttle aggressiveness.

### 3) Safety
- Same-UID by default.
- Protected process hard-block.
- Explainability: ledger must show why throttle was chosen and the expected relief.

### 4) Verification
- Verify throttle settings changed (read-back) and record outcome.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] CPU throttle works on supported systems.
- [ ] Reversal metadata is captured.
- [ ] Fallback behavior is deterministic and logged.

## Test Plan
- Integration: throttle a known CPU burner in an isolated cgroup and confirm CPU usage drops.
- Negative tests: permission denied/unavailable controller.
- Logging: ensure before/after cpu.max values appear in outcomes.
","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"Dicklesworthstone","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-15T10:03:25.474052813Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-15T21:24:48.717285140Z","compaction_level":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"process_triage-sj6.6","depends_on_id":"process_triage-3nz","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-15T10:12:48.267175751Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","metadata":"","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"process_triage-sj6.6","depends_on_id":"process_triage-cfon.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-15T10:12:48.185560767Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","metadata":"","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"process_triage-sj6.6","depends_on_id":"process_triage-qa9","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-15T10:12:48.100028247Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","metadata":"","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"process_triage-sj6.6","depends_on_id":"process_triage-sj6","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-15T10:03:25.475356051Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","metadata":"","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"process_triage-sj6.6","depends_on_id":"process_triage-urd","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-15T10:12:48.016809095Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","metadata":"","thread_id":""}]}

- Negative tests: permission denied/unavailable controller.
- Logging: ensure before/after cpu.max values appear in outcomes.

 {"id":"process_triage-sj6.7","title":"Implement cpuset quarantine action (cpuset controller)","description":"## Overview
Implement the **cpuset quarantine** action from Plan §6:
- constrain a workload to a limited CPU set (and possibly NUMA nodes) to reduce system-wide impact.

This is an advanced containment action that must be conservative and opt-in.

## Requirements
### 1) Capability detection
- Detect cpuset controller availability (cgroup v2 cpuset or v1 cpuset).
- Verify write permissions.

### 2) Action semantics
- Create/select a quarantine cpuset and move the target process group/workload into it.
- Capture previous cpuset membership for reversibility.

### 3) Safety gates
- Protected denylist hard-block.
- Ensure quarantine does not starve the workload in a way that causes deadlocks (policy min CPU count).
- Never apply by default in robot mode unless explicitly allowed.

### 4) Verification
- Verify cpuset assignment changed and log outcomes.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Cpuset quarantine works when supported/allowed.
- [ ] Reversal metadata is captured.
- [ ] Safety gates prevent unsafe cpuset assignments.

## Test Plan
- Integration: in a cpuset-capable test environment, quarantine a CPU burner and verify affinity changes.
- Negative: controller missing/permission denied.
- Logging: verify before/after cpuset assignments captured.

 {"id":"process_triage-sj6.8","title":"Implement supervisor-aware action executors (systemd/launchd/pm2/docker/supervisord)","description":"## Overview
Implement execution support in the action system for **supervisor-aware actions** produced by supervisor detection (Plan §6.1, Phase 10).

Examples:
- `systemctl stop/restart/reload/mask <unit>`
- `launchctl stop/bootout/kickstart <label>`
- `pm2 stop/restart/delete <name>`
- `docker stop/restart/pause/kill <container>`
- `supervisorctl stop/restart <name>`

## Requirements
### 1) Action type support
- Extend the action plan schema to represent supervisor actions as first-class action types (not ad-hoc strings).
- Include fields:
  - supervisor type
  - unit/label/container/id
  - command to run (for human review) + structured parameters (for safe execution)

### 2) Execution
- Execute supervisor actions via a controlled command runner:
  - timeouts
  - output size caps
  - capture stdout/stderr for telemetry
  - structured failure type mapping (`permission_denied`, `timeout`, `supervisor_conflict`, etc.)

### 3) Safety gates
- Default same-UID applies, but note:
  - some supervisor actions require elevated privileges
  - robot mode must not auto-escalate to sudo by default
- Enforce protected patterns and session safety.

### 4) Verification
- Verify the intended outcome (process stopped/restarted) via re-scan/identity checks.
- Detect respawn loops and route to recovery tree.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Supervisor actions are represented as structured plan actions.
- [ ] Execution is bounded (timeouts/caps) and fully logged.
- [ ] Verification correctly detects stop/restart success and respawn loops.

## Test Plan
- Unit: command rendering and parameter validation per supervisor type.
- Integration: mocked supervisor CLIs (or test containers) producing deterministic outputs.
- E2E: plan includes supervisor stop for a supervised process and records outcomes.
- Logging: verify captured stdout/stderr and structured failure types.

 {"id":"process_triage-sj6.9","title":"Implement session safety protections (active TTY/ssh/tmux chains) in action planning/execution","description":"## Overview
Implement the plan’s **session safety** invariant (Plan §6 “Session safety”): protect the active login/session chain so pt never cuts off the user running it.

This applies both to plan generation (recommendations) and to action execution (hard blocks).

## Requirements
### 1) Identify protected session chain
Best-effort detection of:
- current controlling TTY
- parent shell(s)
- tmux/screen server + client PIDs
- ssh server/client chain when present

### 2) Enforce protections
- Default policy: processes in the protected chain are not eligible for destructive actions.
- Robot mode must never override without explicit policy allowlist.
- Explainability: clearly label “protected: active session chain” in UI/agent outputs.

### 3) Integration points
- Plan generation must mark session-chain processes as KEEP/protected.
- Action execution must re-check protection at apply time.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Protected session chain is detected in common setups (shell, tmux, ssh).
- [ ] Protected processes are hard-blocked from kill/pause/freeze by default.
- [ ] UI/agent outputs clearly show protection reason.

## Test Plan
- Integration: run tests inside a PTY/tmux-like harness and validate protections.
- Unit: fixture ancestry graphs representing ssh/tmux chains.
- Logging: ensure protection decisions are auditable.
